questions.q: The Agni missile is developed by which Indian organisation?
questions.explanation: The Agni family of ballistic missiles is developed by the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO).

questions.q: What type of weapon is the Agni?
questions.explanation: Agni is a family of surface-to-surface ballistic missiles forming a pillar of India's strategic deterrent.

questions.q: The word 'Agni' is a Sanskrit word meaning what?
questions.explanation: 'Agni' means 'fire' in Sanskrit, also the name of the Vedic fire deity.

questions.q: The Agni programme was developed under which major Indian missile development initiative launched in the 1980s?
questions.explanation: Agni was one of the missiles developed under the Integrated Guided Missile Development Programme (IGMDP) started in 1983.

questions.q: Which scientist, later President of India, is closely associated with leading India's missile programmes including Agni?
questions.explanation: A. P. J. Abdul Kalam led the IGMDP and is popularly called the 'Missile Man of India'.

questions.q: The Agni missiles are primarily designed to deliver what kind of payload?
questions.explanation: Agni missiles are capable of carrying nuclear as well as conventional warheads, central to India's deterrent.

questions.q: What characteristic of the Agni missiles is emphasised by the card's subtitle 'India's Long Reach'?
questions.explanation: The Agni family provides India with long-range strike capability, hence 'India's Long Reach'.

questions.q: In which decade did the first Agni missile have its initial test flight?
questions.explanation: Agni-I (Agni Technology Demonstrator) was first test-fired in 1989, during the 1980s.

questions.q: Agni missiles are a key part of which aspect of India's defence posture?
questions.explanation: The Agni series is a pillar of India's strategic nuclear deterrent.

questions.q: Which Indian armed forces command operates the Agni nuclear-capable missiles?
questions.explanation: India's Strategic Forces Command (SFC) is responsible for the nuclear-capable Agni missiles.

questions.q: Which Agni variant is widely regarded as India's first intercontinental-range ballistic missile with a range of about 5,000 km or more?
questions.explanation: Agni-V has a range exceeding 5,000 km, giving India intercontinental-range capability.

questions.q: In which year was the Agni-V first successfully test-fired?
questions.explanation: Agni-V was first successfully flight-tested on 19 April 2012.

questions.q: The original Agni Technology Demonstrator (Agni-I prototype) of 1989 used a combination of stages drawn from which two existing programmes?
options: A SLV-3 first stage with a Prithvi-derived second stage
A PSLV booster with a cruise missile
questions.explanation: The 1989 Agni demonstrator combined a solid SLV-3 derived first stage with a Prithvi-derived liquid second stage.

questions.q: Which Agni variant is a newer canisterised, rail- and road-mobile, two-stage missile with a range of roughly 1,000-2,000 km, first tested in 2021?
questions.explanation: Agni-P (Agni-Prime) is a newer canisterised medium-range missile with a range of about 1,000-2,000 km, first tested in June 2021.

questions.q: Roughly what is the range class of the Agni-III missile?
questions.explanation: Agni-III is an intermediate-range ballistic missile with a range of roughly 3,000-5,000 km.

questions.q: What propulsion type do the operational Agni missiles primarily use in their later, fully solid versions?
questions.explanation: Modern Agni variants (Agni-II onward) use solid-propellant stages, allowing faster readiness and easier handling.

questions.q: Agni-V is reported to be capable of carrying MIRVs. What does MIRV stand for?
questions.explanation: MIRV stands for Multiple Independently-targetable Re-entry Vehicles, allowing one missile to strike several targets.

questions.q: In March 2024 DRDO announced the first successful flight test of Agni-V equipped with MIRV technology. What was the codename of that mission?
questions.explanation: The maiden MIRV-capable Agni-V test in March 2024 was named Mission Divyastra.

questions.q: Agni-V is canisterised, meaning the missile is stored and launched from a sealed container. What is a key operational advantage of this?
options: It increases the missile's blast yield
It enables faster launch readiness and easier transport/storage
It makes the missile lighter than air
It removes the need for any guidance system
questions.explanation: Canisterisation seals the missile for long storage and allows quick launch readiness and mobile deployment.

questions.q: From which site off the Odisha coast are Agni missiles typically test-launched?
options: Dr. A. P. J. Abdul Kalam Island (Wheeler Island)
questions.explanation: Agni tests are usually conducted from the Integrated Test Range at Dr. A. P. J. Abdul Kalam Island (formerly Wheeler Island), Odisha.

questions.q: Which guidance technology is central to the Agni missiles' ability to reach their targets accurately?
questions.explanation: Agni missiles rely on inertial navigation systems, with later variants adding ring-laser gyros and satellite updates for accuracy.

questions.q: Agni-II, an upgrade over Agni-I, is generally classed as which type of ballistic missile by range?
questions.explanation: Agni-II is a medium/intermediate-range ballistic missile with a range of around 2,000-3,000 km.

questions.q: Agni-I is generally classified as which type of ballistic missile, with a range of roughly 700-900 km?
questions.explanation: The operational Agni-I is a single-stage, road/rail-mobile short-range ballistic missile (~700-900 km).

questions.q: Agni-IV (initially referred to during development by another designation) is a two-stage missile with a range of about 3,500-4,000 km. By what earlier name was it sometimes referred during development?
questions.explanation: Agni-IV was during development referred to as 'Agni-II Prime' before being officially designated Agni-IV.

questions.q: What technology demonstrated on Agni reentry vehicles allows them to survive the extreme heat of atmospheric reentry?
options: Ceramic tiles identical to the Space Shuttle
questions.explanation: Agni reentry vehicles use indigenously developed carbon-carbon composite heat shields to withstand reentry temperatures.

questions.q: Which later Agni variant introduced a compact ring-laser gyroscope-based inertial navigation system and a micro navigation system to improve accuracy?
questions.explanation: Agni-IV introduced advanced avionics including a ring-laser gyro inertial navigation system and micro navigation system.

questions.q: Reports describe a planned/future Agni variant aimed at even greater intercontinental ranges (over 10,000 km). By what name has this proposed missile sometimes been referred?
questions.explanation: Agni-VI is the reported future variant intended for ranges beyond Agni-V, with development discussed by DRDO.

questions.q: Agni-V's first 2012 test flight reached intercontinental range largely because it used how many stages of solid propulsion?
questions.explanation: Agni-V is a three-stage, all-solid-fuel missile, enabling its 5,000+ km range.

questions.q: Under the IGMDP launched in 1983, Agni was developed alongside several other missiles. Which of the following was NOT one of the original IGMDP missiles?
questions.explanation: The original IGMDP missiles were Prithvi, Agni, Akash, Trishul and Nag; BrahMos was a later India-Russia joint venture, not part of IGMDP.

questions.q: Why is the Agni-V's road-mobile, canisterised configuration considered significant for India's deterrence doctrine of credible minimum deterrence?
options: It makes the missile cheaper to build than a satellite
It enhances survivability and second-strike capability by being hard to locate and quick to launch
It allows the missile to be launched from aircraft only
It removes the need for a warhead
questions.explanation: A mobile, canisterised missile is harder to detect and pre-empt, strengthening survivable second-strike capability under credible minimum deterrence.
